skill.name: scientific-slides
skill.description: Create scientific presentation decks for conferences, seminars, thesis defenses, and research talks. The skill provides deck structure, slide design guidance, timing advice, Beamer templates, PowerPoint guidance, visual review workflows, and optional AI image generation scripts.
skill.summary: Create structured, visual research presentations for scientific talks.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: AI review did not find confirmed malicious intent or prompt injection, but several high-risk implementation patterns are real. The skill includes executable scripts that handle API keys, load .env files from parent directories, send prompts and attached images to OpenRouter, write local files, and run pdflatex on presentation input. Many static blocker hits for weak cryptography, C2 keywords, SAM, and shell backticks are false positives from Markdown, LaTeX, and presentation examples.

high_findings.title: API Key Passed Through Process Arguments
high_findings.description: The wrapper reads OPENROUTER_API_KEY or --api-key, then appends the secret to a subprocess command line. Command-line arguments can be exposed through process listings or logs on shared systems.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly adds the API key value to cmd before subprocess.run. This is a concrete credential exposure risk, even though the intended use is legitimate.

high_findings.title: External AI Service Receives User Prompts and Attached Images
high_findings.description: The AI generation script builds OpenRouter API requests containing the user prompt and any attached image data. This can disclose unpublished figures, lab data, or proprietary material to an external provider.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network destination and request construction are explicit. The behavior is expected for AI image generation, but it is a real privacy and data handling risk.

high_findings.title: Parent Directory Environment File Loading
high_findings.description: The script searches current and parent directories for .env files before loading OPENROUTER_API_KEY. This may unintentionally load secrets from unrelated projects or higher-level directories.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent traversal and dotenv loading are visible in code. The behavior is not malicious by itself, but the broad search scope increases accidental secret exposure risk.

high_findings.title: Local LaTeX Compilation on Presentation Input
high_findings.description: The validator runs pdflatex against the target presentation directory. Compiling untrusted TeX can trigger file reads, writes, and TeX engine behaviors outside simple validation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is direct and uses a fixed executable with a timeout. The command is legitimate for validation, but it is high risk when applied to untrusted LaTeX sources.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Output File Writes
medium_findings.description: The scripts create output directories and write generated images, PDFs, or rendered slide images to user-provided paths. This is expected functionality, but it can overwrite files if paths are chosen carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and tied to user-supplied output paths. This is normal for conversion tools, but it should be documented for users.

medium_findings.title: Base64 Image Processing Is Legitimate but Sensitive
medium_findings.description: The static obfuscation findings are false positives for malware intent. The base64 code decodes image data from the API and encodes attached images for API requests, which can still expose sensitive image content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Base64 use is semantically image transport, not code hiding. The residual concern is privacy because attached local images are sent to the external API.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Recommends Installing and Running External Tools
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes shell commands for installing ImageMagick and running conversion scripts. These commands are ordinary presentation workflow examples, but users should review commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in documentation and are relevant to visual review. The sudo install example is a real privileged command, but it is not hidden or automatically executed.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and API Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs point to OpenRouter, example websites, or user-facing documentation links. No evidence found that these URLs are covert command-and-control endpoints.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible and align with documented AI generation or placeholder contact use. The network endpoint is still relevant to privacy findings above.

low_findings.title: Static C2, SAM, and Reconnaissance Matches Are Presentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner matched presentation terms such as TAM, SAM, SOM and ordinary design or workflow language. No evidence found of Windows SAM access, C2 behavior, or reconnaissance logic in those locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review showed business and installation examples, not credential database access or command-and-control content. Confidence is high for these cited examples, though not every static hit was individually re-read.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Mostly Non-Cryptographic Text
low_findings.description: The numerous weak cryptography findings appear to come from Markdown, LaTeX math, abbreviations, and presentation examples rather than cryptographic implementations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or RC4 being used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted context around representative hits was benign. Confidence is moderate-high because the files are presentation materials, but the static result list is too large for exhaustive line-by-line confirmation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Plus Credentials Plus File Attachments
dangerous_patterns.description: The image generator combines credential access, external HTTP requests, and optional local file attachment encoding. This is legitimate for the feature, but it is a sensitive data flow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The combined data flow is explicit in the script. I did not find evidence of covert exfiltration beyond the documented OpenRouter workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Execution for Local Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill uses subprocess execution for the AI wrapper and LaTeX validation. Arguments are structured lists, reducing shell injection risk, but the commands still execute local tools.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct and easy to verify. They are not shell=True injection patterns, but local execution remains a marketplace risk factor.

content.value_statement: Researchers need clear presentations that explain complex work without crowded slides. This skill guides structure, visual design, timing, review, and optional AI-assisted slide image generation.
actual_capabilities: Plans scientific slide decks for conferences, seminars, thesis defenses, journal clubs, and grant pitches.
Provides guidance for narrative structure, timing, audience fit, and speaker preparation.
Includes LaTeX Beamer templates for conference, seminar, and defense presentations.
Documents PowerPoint design principles for scientific figures, data slides, and visual hierarchy.
Includes scripts to generate AI slide images, combine slide images into PDF, convert PDFs to images, and validate presentations.
Provides visual review workflows for checking readability, layout, contrast, and slide consistency.
limitations: AI image generation requires an OpenRouter API key and sends prompts and attached images to an external service.
Generated slides still require expert review for scientific accuracy, citations, and data interpretation.
LaTeX, PowerPoint, Pillow, PyMuPDF, and related tools may need local installation for some workflows.
The included scripts can read local files, write outputs, and run local presentation tools.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Conference Talk
use_cases.description: Turn a paper or project summary into a timed conference deck with clear motivation, methods, results, and takeaway slides.

use_cases.title: Build a Thesis Defense
use_cases.description: Plan a defense presentation with background, research questions, chapters, contributions, limitations, and backup slides.

use_cases.title: Review Scientific Visuals
use_cases.description: Check slide images and presentation files for readability, visual consistency, timing, and audience fit before a talk.

prompt_templates.title: Outline a Talk
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 12-slide outline for a 15-minute conference talk about my research topic. Include slide titles, purpose, timing, and suggested visuals.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Existing Slides
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current scientific slide deck for narrative flow, crowded slides, figure clarity, and timing. Suggest concrete edits slide by slide.

prompt_templates.title: Design Results Slides
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design results slides from these figures. Identify the main claim for each slide, supporting evidence, visual emphasis, and concise speaker prompts.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Defense Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a thesis defense deck plan for a 45-minute talk. Include narrative sections, slide counts, transitions, backup slides, and review checkpoints.

output_examples.input: I need a 10-minute conference presentation about a new data analysis method.
output: A timed deck outline with title, motivation, problem, method, validation, results, limitations, and conclusion slides.
Suggested visuals for each slide, such as workflow diagrams, example datasets, and one main results figure.
Advice to reduce text and keep one scientific claim per slide.

output_examples.input: Review my thesis defense slides for clarity and timing.
output: A slide-by-slide review of narrative flow, audience assumptions, figure readability, and timing risks.
Recommendations for moving details to backup slides and strengthening transitions between research chapters.
A checklist for final rehearsal and visual inspection.

output_examples.input: Create Beamer slides for a seminar on microscopy results.
output: A seminar structure with context, methods, representative images, quantitative results, interpretation, and discussion prompts.
Guidance for choosing a Beamer template and adapting title, section, results, and backup slides.
Notes on citation placement, figure captions, and readable projected typography.

best_practices: Start with audience, time limit, and one main message before choosing slide layouts.
Use one scientific claim per slide and support it with the clearest figure or diagram.
Run a visual review pass after export to check readability, contrast, cropping, and timing.
anti_patterns: Do not paste manuscript paragraphs directly into slides.
Do not generate final slides without verifying citations, data labels, and scientific claims.
Do not attach sensitive unpublished data to external AI services without permission.

faq.question: Can this skill create both PowerPoint and Beamer presentations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for PowerPoint workflows and LaTeX Beamer templates for several scientific talk types.

faq.question: Does it generate complete slide images?
faq.answer: Yes, optional scripts can generate slide images through OpenRouter when an API key is available.

faq.question: Can I use it without external AI image generation?
faq.answer: Yes. The structure guides, design references, timing guidance, and Beamer templates can be used without network calls.

faq.question: Is it suitable for thesis defenses?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes defense-oriented guidance for long-form structure, background, contributions, and backup slides.

faq.question: Does it verify scientific correctness?
faq.answer: No. It helps with presentation structure and design, but domain experts must verify claims, methods, data, and citations.

faq.question: What security caution should users know?
faq.answer: The scripts can use API keys, upload prompts or attached images, write files, and compile LaTeX locally.
